title: Run 18: sentence-level decomposition from verbatim abstracts
description: Inherits the Run 17 transparent multi-agent loop (reasoning_trace on every decision; 4-gate deterministic filter UNCHANGED; AGENT 5 reasoning-auditor) and changes the SOURCING METHOD: atoms are extracted by decomposing the VERBATIM ABSTRACT that WebSearch returns for an arxiv paper (NOT WebFetch, which 403s) into sentence/clause-level sub-mechanisms. A new per-atom saturation step measures how many papers EACH sub-mechanism alone returns; the merger then pairs the SPARSEST sub-mechanisms (lowest combined paper-hits) to test the KEY QUESTION (R12): at sentence-level granularity, does any sub-mechanism live in sparse (<10 paper) space, and does pairing sparse atoms produce lower per-candidate paper-hits than Run 17's 30-38 (which would let Gate-1 novelty clear)?

sourcing_method.rule: R9: source atom text by decomposing the VERBATIM abstract WebSearch returns for 'arxiv <id> <title> abstract'. WebFetch 403s on arxiv/HF/alphaxiv (verified Run 17) -- do NOT rely on it.
sourcing_method.note: The search subsystem renders abstracts as clean text and may convert first-person ('we propose') to third-person ('the paper proposes'); the mechanism content is recorded verbatim as seen this session. Each distinct mechanism sentence/clause becomes a SEPARATE atom.

agents.task: decompose 3 verbatim abstracts into sentence-level atoms; reasoning_trace per atom (which sentence, why distinct)
1 WebSearch per atom on that sub-mechanism alone; record verbatim paper-hit count + results; flag sparse (<10) -- R10
pick top 5 CROSS-PAPER pairs by LOWEST combined paper-hits; Opus merge each (reasoning_trace per merge)
5 reformulations/candidate + independent re-check; reasoning_trace per step (R7)
audit ALL reasoning_traces; flag logic breaks (R10)

merge_schema.primary_quote: verbatim >=30-char substring of atom_a or atom_b text

pair_selection.rule: eligible pairs = atoms from DIFFERENT source papers (same-paper sub-mechanisms already co-occur, so they are trivially non-novel); rank eligible pairs by combined per-atom paper-hits ASC; take the 5 lowest.
pair_selection.tie_break: lower max single-atom hits, then atom_id lexicographic (deterministic)

gates.gate_4: Belinda strict (mechanism causal verb + >=30-char verbatim quote substring)

key_analysis.R10: report per-atom paper-hit counts explicitly (the new data)
key_analysis.R12: did pairing SPARSE sub-mechanism atoms produce LOWER per-candidate paper-hits than Run 17's 30-38? Yes => pairing sparse atoms may clear Gate 1 (NICHE_FOUND possible). No => saturation holds even at sentence-level granularity. Report honestly either way; goal is the honest answer + full transparency, not the verdict.

hard_rules.R1: agents SEQUENTIAL, each commits+pushes before next (branch claude/run-18)
hard_rules.R2: push after every agent; if push fails -> STOP+report
hard_rules.R4: force raw JSON from Opus, 1 retry
hard_rules.R5: record ONLY web_search results actually seen -- never fabricate
hard_rules.R7: AGENT 4 independently re-verifies; AGENT 5 audits all reasoning logic
hard_rules.R9: source atoms from WebSearch VERBATIM abstracts (fetch is 403 -- don't rely on it)
hard_rules.R10: report per-atom paper-hit counts explicitly (the key new data)
hard_rules.R11: if session long -> commit partial + report
hard_rules.R12: KEY QUESTION: at sentence-level granularity, does ANY sub-mechanism live in sparse (<10 paper) space, and does pairing sparse atoms beat Gate 1? Report honestly whichever way.
